message.content:
Adversaries may create or modify launch agents to ensure the repeated execution of malicious payloads, achieving persistence on macOS systems. Launch agents are configured via property list (.plist) files located in directories such as /System/Library/LaunchAgents, /Library/LaunchAgents, and ~/Library/LaunchAgents. These files use keys like Label, ProgramArguments, and RunAtLoad to define the agent's behavior. By placing a .plist file with the RunAtLoad or KeepAlive keys set to true, adversaries can ensure their malicious code runs at user login. Commonly, adversaries disguise these agents with names mimicking legitimate system or software processes.

The impact of this technique includes sustained unauthorized access and control over the compromised system, often used for activities like cryptocurrency mining, data exfiltration, or further malware deployment. Notable malware families exploiting this technique include Bundlore, Calisto, CoinTicker, CookieMiner, CrossRAT, and others.

Mitigation strategies include monitoring and validating the integrity of launch agent directories, employing endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect suspicious .plist file modifications, and educating users about the risks of unauthorized software installations. Regular audits and the use of application whitelisting can also help prevent unauthorized persistence mechanisms.
